description: Video creative metrics (hook/hold/thruplay) and engagement-quality metrics. Hook and hold rates are the strongest leading indicators of creative health for short-form Meta video.

hook_rate.name: Hook Rate (Thumbstop Rate)
hook_rate.definition: Percentage of impressions where the viewer watched at least the first 3 seconds — i.e. the opening stopped the scroll. Motion treats thumbstop rate and hook rate as interchangeable.
hook_rate.meaning: Measures how well the first 3 seconds arrest attention. The single most predictive early signal of short-form video performance.
hook_rate.warning: Hook rate below 20% — the opening is not stopping enough scrolls.
hook_rate.critical: Hook rate below 15% — rework the opening 3 seconds before spending more.
possible_causes: No pattern interrupt or motion in first second
recommendations: Lead with motion, faces, or a bold claim in second one
Cut any intro/branding before the hook
Open on the problem or payoff, not setup
best_practices: Judge hooks at equal spend/impressions, not raw views
common_mistakes: Reusing one hook across all variations
notes_edge_cases: 3-second plays count auto-played muted views; pair with hold rate to confirm genuine attention.

hold_rate.name: Hold Rate
hold_rate.definition: The share of viewers retained beyond the initial hook into the body of the video — sustained attention after the opening.
hold_rate.meaning: Whether the creative keeps attention after the hook. A good hook with poor hold means the promise is not paid off.
hold_rate.warning: Hold rate below 20% — viewers drop after the hook.
hold_rate.critical: Hold rate below 15% with a strong hook — message body is failing.
possible_causes: Message buried or CTA too late
Length exceeds attention for the offer
recommendations: Pay off the hook's promise immediately
Move key message and CTA earlier
best_practices: Optimise hook and hold together — both must be healthy
Use retention curves to find the drop-off frame
common_mistakes: Making videos longer than the story needs
notes_edge_cases: 15-30s videos achieve the highest thruplay/hold; 60s+ retains markedly less.

thruplay_rate.name: ThruPlay Rate
thruplay_rate.definition: Percentage of impressions counted as a ThruPlay — the video was watched to completion, or for at least 15 seconds (whichever comes first).
thruplay_rate.meaning: Meta's standard 'meaningful view' metric and a common video optimisation event.
thruplay_rate.critical: ThruPlay rate below 12% on a video-views objective.
recommendations: Strengthen hook and hold first (they drive thruplay)
Optimise directly for ThruPlay when goal is views
best_practices: Track cost per ThruPlay for video-views budgets
common_mistakes: Optimising thruplay for a conversion goal where it is a vanity proxy
notes_edge_cases: Cost per ThruPlay typically ranges $0.01-$0.10 depending on audience and creative.

cost_per_thruplay.name: Cost Per ThruPlay
cost_per_thruplay.definition: Average spend per ThruPlay (15s or completion).
cost_per_thruplay.meaning: Efficiency of buying meaningful video views; the headline cost metric for video-views objectives.
best_practices: Use only for awareness/views goals, never as a conversion proxy
common_mistakes: Treating cheap thruplays as business value

video_avg_watch_time.name: Average Watch Time / Average Play Time
video_avg_watch_time.definition: Average duration the video was played per impression.
video_avg_watch_time.meaning: Depth of engagement with video content; complements hold rate.

benchmark.note: Relative to video length; compare against the asset's own length and prior versions.

video_avg_watch_time.warning: Average watch time well under the point where the CTA appears.
video_avg_watch_time.critical: Average watch time under 3s (audience not even hooked).
recommendations: Move CTA before the average drop-off point
best_practices: Place key message before the average watch-time mark
common_mistakes: Putting the CTA after most viewers have left

engagement_rate.name: Engagement Rate
engagement_rate.definition: Share of people who interacted with the ad (reactions, comments, shares, saves, clicks) relative to reach or impressions. Meta: higher engagement increases clicks, conversions, and revenue.
engagement_rate.meaning: Signals creative resonance and social proof, which also feeds relevance ranking and lowers delivery cost.

benchmark.note: Varies widely by format and objective; benchmark against the account's own norms.

engagement_rate.warning: Engagement falling while frequency rises (early fatigue).
engagement_rate.critical: Near-zero engagement on a funded ad (creative or audience mismatch).
best_practices: Treat strong organic engagement as a signal to scale that creative
Use engagement as a leading, not lagging, KPI
common_mistakes: Chasing vanity engagement detached from conversions
notes_edge_cases: High comment volume can be negative sentiment — read quality, not just quantity.

creative_fatigue.name: Creative Fatigue
creative_fatigue.definition: Performance decay of an ad over time as the audience becomes saturated by repeated exposure. Detected by combining signals, not one metric.
creative_fatigue.formula: signature: frequency rising AND CTR declining AND CPA/CPM climbing over the same window
creative_fatigue.meaning: The dominant cause of gradual decline in an otherwise winning ad. Diagnosed by the joint movement of frequency, CTR, and cost.

benchmark.trigger: frequency >3-4 (cold) with >20-30% CTR drop from baseline

creative_fatigue.warning: Two of three signals present (e.g. frequency up + CTR down) without cost impact yet.
creative_fatigue.critical: All three present: frequency up, CTR down, CPA up.
recommendations: Reduce budget concentration on the fatigued ad
best_practices: Maintain a creative pipeline / refresh cadence
Watch the three-signal signature, not any single metric
common_mistakes: Reacting to a single soft day
Increasing budget on a fatigued ad, accelerating decay
notes_edge_cases: Adlytic's PatternEngine encodes this as the DYING_CREATIVE pattern signature.

relevance_ranking.name: Ad Relevance Diagnostics (Quality / Engagement / Conversion Ranking)
relevance_ranking.definition: Meta's three relevance diagnostics ranking an ad against ads competing for the same audience: Quality Ranking, Engagement Rate Ranking, and Conversion Rate Ranking (Above Average / Average / Below Average).
relevance_ranking.formula: relative percentile vs competing ads for the same audience
relevance_ranking.meaning: Explains why delivery is expensive: low rankings raise CPM and suppress reach. A diagnostic, not a score to optimise directly.

benchmark.target: Average or Above Average on all three

relevance_ranking.critical: Quality and Conversion ranking both Below Average (expensive, poorly converting delivery).
best_practices: Use the three rankings to localise the problem (creative vs engagement vs post-click)
common_mistakes: Treating rankings as a single score
Ignoring conversion ranking while fixating on quality
notes_edge_cases: Rankings are relative — a good ad can rank low simply against very strong competitors.
